title: Research Question & Literature Review - Multiple Choice

questions.question: A student proposes this research question: "How accurately can a machine learning model predict moment-to-moment attentional lapses in everyday environments using continuous EEG recordings collected over a five-year period?" The RQ contains a flaw. Which revision best fixes its main issue?

options.A: How does sustained attention feel to people across different everyday environments over many years of life?
options.B: How accurately can models predict attentional lapses from EEG recorded during laboratory attention tasks?
options.C: What is the deeper neural meaning of attention in the human brain across the entire human lifespan and beyond?
options.D: How accurately can one model predict attentional lapses everywhere using lifelong EEG gathered from many people?

questions.explanation: The flaw is infeasible scope: five years of continuous everyday-environment EEG cannot be done in a student project. B narrows it to a feasible, measurable lab-task study while keeping the same construct. A changes the construct to subjective feeling and is untestable; C is broad and philosophical; D stays just as infeasible and vague.

questions.question: A draft introduction supports a single claim with a string of eight in-text citations packed into one sentence. A reviewer flags it. What is the main problem?

options.A: Too many sources support one claim; cite at most about three, keeping the strongest two or three.
options.B: It wrongly mixes in-text and parenthetical citation styles, and that is the real error to fix here.
options.C: It gives far too many concrete numerical examples for what is only an introduction paragraph here.
options.D: At least one of the cited sources is almost certainly too old to belong in a modern introduction now.

questions.explanation: Overciting confuses and distracts the reader; pick the two or three strongest sources for one claim. B, C, and D are plausible writing concerns but none is the actual issue here, which is simply citing too many sources at once.

questions.question: A peer claims that a literature review is essentially a well-organised summary of every paper you read. Is this RIGHT or WRONG, and why?

options.A: RIGHT, because the reader needs an accurate description of each source you consulted.
options.B: RIGHT, as long as the summaries are grouped neatly by author for easy reference.
options.C: WRONG, because a review is analysis and critical evaluation that shows the gap your RQ fills.
options.D: WRONG, because a review should instead list only the methods sections of each paper.

questions.explanation: A lit review is not a summary; it critically evaluates and connects sources to motivate your research gap ("joining the conversation"). A and B treat it as description; D invents a narrow methods-only rule that the section content does not support.

questions.question: Belcher (2019) frames academic writing as a "debate you overheard." What does this imply for structuring a literature review?

options.A: You should review the sides of the ongoing debate, not summarise sources one by one.
options.B: You should report only the loudest single source and treat its lone view as fully settled fact.
options.C: You should avoid citing any prior work at all so that your own voice stays clearly dominant throughout.
options.D: You should present the sources strictly in the chronological order in which you happened to read them.

questions.explanation: "Joining the conversation" means entering an ongoing scholarly debate and organising the review around its sides, which avoids the summary effect. B over-trusts one source; C contradicts the point of a review; D imposes a meaningless reading-order structure.

questions.question: A student writes: "Background noise reduces task performance because divided attention lowers accuracy." Which statement correctly distinguishes a research question from a hypothesis here?

options.A: Both sentences are hypotheses, since any sentence about variables predicts an outcome.
options.B: The sentence is a research question, because it ends without a question mark by mistake.
options.C: It is a hypothesis: "Does background noise reduce task performance?" would be the RQ.
options.D: It is neither, because hypotheses may never mention a proposed mechanism such as attention.

questions.explanation: The RQ is the question; the hypothesis is your predicted answer. "Does noise reduce performance?" is the question, while the given sentence asserts a predicted outcome and so is a hypothesis. A erases the distinction, B fixates on punctuation, and D invents a false rule about mechanisms.

questions.question: A student needs a definition of the "cocktail-party effect" and cites a 2021 textbook that itself attributes the term to Cherry (1953). What citation mistake is this, and what should they do?

options.A: Misattribution; they should credit the textbook authors as the originators of the effect.
options.B: Citing the citation; they should track down and cite Cherry (1953), the original source.
options.C: Overciting a definition; they should drop the citation and state the definition unsourced.
options.D: Citing irrelevant literature; the cocktail-party effect does not belong in attention work.
options.E: No mistake; citing whichever recent source mentions the term is always acceptable.

questions.explanation: Citing a paper that cites the original instead of the original itself is "citing the citation"; always find the original source for a definition (here Cherry, 1953). A reverses the credit, C wrongly drops attribution, and D mislabels a clearly relevant concept as irrelevant.

questions.question: Why is academic reading described as goal-directed rather than read top-to-bottom like a novel?

options.A: Because journal articles are legally required to be skimmed, never read in full.
options.B: Because reading every word in linear order is the surest way to avoid any misinterpretation.
options.C: Because the abstract is always wrong, so the article body must be read first instead.
options.D: Because you hunt for what serves your goal, using different strategies for different aims.

questions.explanation: You read with a specific goal, hunting for what you need and switching strategies (skim for relevance, deep-read for argument). A invents a legal rule, B describes novel-reading (the very contrast being rejected), and C wrongly distrusts abstracts.

questions.question: What is the course policy on using generative AI to write text, and the main reason behind it?

options.A: GenAI is fully banned for all tasks because it can never improve a sentence's clarity.
options.B: GenAI text may be submitted verbatim as long as a reference manager formats it.
options.C: Avoid fully GenAI-generated text, because models hallucinate references and content.
options.D: GenAI is encouraged for final drafts since its citations are more reliable than yours.

questions.explanation: The policy is to avoid fully GenAI-generated text because models hallucinate references and their content, and passing off an LLM's words as your own is plagiarism. A overstates the ban, B ignores plagiarism and hallucination, and D falsely claims GenAI citations are reliable.

questions.question: A student is deciding whether a found source is scholarly. Which combination of signals best supports judging it as scholarly?

options.A: Peer review, recognised author credentials, and a reputable publication venue.
options.B: A confident tone, a long word count, and a recent posting date on a blog.
options.C: High view counts, a catchy headline, and inclusion in a Google web search.
options.D: A paywall, a downloadable PDF, and the presence of any reference list at all.

questions.explanation: Scholarly status rests on peer review, author credentials, and the venue. B, C, and D list surface features (tone, popularity, format) that non-scholarly sources can easily share, so they do not establish scholarliness.

questions.question: A reviewer says a lit-review draft suffers from the "summary effect." Which revision best addresses it?

options.A: Add a one-paragraph summary of each study, ordered alphabetically by first author.
options.B: Move every individual study summary into a single long table at the end of the section.
options.C: Cut all citations and replace them with the student's own opinions about the topic.
options.D: Group studies into sub-themes or sides of a debate that build toward the research gap.

questions.explanation: The summary effect is fixed by organising sources by topic or debate side rather than author, which facilitates comparison and guides the reader to your gap. A keeps author-by-author summarising, B just relocates the summaries without analysis, and C removes the evidence base.
